methods.name: Stage-Progression

methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Biotech & Health, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Altos Labs is a late-stage biotech company founded by Hal Barron, Rick Klausner, and Hans Bishop that has raised $3.0B to date. The valuation universe for Biotech & Health includes 3 public comparables (RXRX, TEM, ONT.L) and 8 private peers, though none of the private peers share sufficient stage and valuation-tier overlap to support median peer benchmarking.

A stage-progression methodology is the primary applicable approach, using total capital raised as a proxy baseline. Applying a 1.2x step-up multiplier typical for late-stage advancement yields an implied valuation of $3.6B. Public biotech comparables trade at a 6.9x price-to-sales median, but this cannot be applied without disclosed revenue figures. The peer-median method is not feasible given the absence of at least three narrow-stage comparables with similar characteristics.

The triangulation supports a valuation range of $3.1B to $4.1B, with a median estimate of $3.6B. This range relies on a single quantitative method and carries material uncertainty around stage-exit multiples in the biotech sector and the absence of revenue data. Key risks include the dependency on capital raised as a valuation proxy in a sector where burn rate and technical milestones are material drivers of value. Additional disclosure of revenue or comparable transaction multiples would sharpen the estimate materially.
